elements.sent-2411-001: This study provides a comprehensive analysis of the ground state and thermodynamic properties of a spin-pseudospin chain representing a model of a one-dimensional dilute magnet with two types of nonmagnetic charged impurities.
elements.sent-2411-002: For this purpose, a method utilizing the transfer-matrix properties is employed.
elements.sent-2411-003: Despite the wide variety of intriguing frustrated phase states, we show that the model showcases pseudotransitions solely between simple charge and magnetic quasiorders.
elements.sent-2411-004: These pseudotransitions are characterized by distinct features in the thermodynamic and magnetic quantities, resembling first- and second-order phase transitions.
elements.sent-2411-005: In addition to pseudotransitions for the ``pure'' system, similar to those observed in other one-dimensional spin models, this study also reveals the presence of ``second-order'' pseudotransitions for the dilute case.
elements.sent-2411-006: We show that the nature of these discovered pseudotransitions is associated with the phase separation in the chain into regions of (anti)ferromagnetic and charge-ordered phases.
elements.sent-2411-007: The ability to compare the results of an exact transfer-matrix calculation with a simple phenomenological description within the framework of Maxwell construction contributes to a deeper understanding of both the physical mechanisms underlying this phenomenon and the analytical methods used.
elements.sent-2411-008: The statistical solvability of one-dimensional models makes it possible to trace in detail the sources of occurrence of certain features of thermodynamic properties.
elements.sent-2411-009: This capability is particularly useful in assessing the applicability of various phenomenological constructions for more complex systems.
elements.sent-2411-010: Despite the well-known rigorous theorems of the absence of traditional phase transitions in one-dimensional models with short-range interactions~\cite{vanHove1950Physica,Cuesta2004JSP , pseudotransitions have been found in a wide class of (quasi)-one-dimensional systems.}
elements.sent-2411-011: They manifest themselves as sharp features of specific heat, susceptibility, and correlation length at the finite temperature.
elements.sent-2411-012: This behavior resembles second-order phase transitions; however, for one-dimensional models, all functions remain continuous and have a finite, albeit very large, maximum value.
elements.sent-2411-013: The experimental detection of pseudotransitions is still under discussion.
elements.sent-2411-014: The search for pseudotransitions in real physical systems is compounded by the challenge that these phenomena exist only within a relatively narrow range of parameters.
elements.sent-2411-015: Although it is theoretically possible to design artificial materials with specified properties, such as through the use of qubits~\cite{OHare2007Aug , this has yet to be successfully implemented.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Nevertheless, the unique features of these phenomena could find a variety of applications~\cite{Yin2024PRR .}
elements.sent-2411-017: In addition, pseudotransitions offer hope of approaching forbidden phase transitions at finite temperatures~\cite{Yin2024PRR,Yin2024PRB .}
elements.sent-2411-018: The existence of a pseudotransition obeys the rule formulated by Rojas~\cite{Rojas2020BJP,*Rojas2020APPA : if the residual entropy is continuous at least from the one-sided limit at the boundary between the ground state phases, a pseudotransition at finite temperature will be observed near this boundary.}
elements.sent-2411-019: At the microscopic level, this means~\cite{Panov2021PRE that the ground state phases with the same energy at the boundary do not form a mixture with higher entropy than those of the neighboring phases.}
elements.sent-2411-020: This is a rather rare situation that imposes significant constraints on the nature of the neighboring phases and makes pseudotransition a rare phenomenon.
elements.sent-2411-021: The high-temperature state in pseudotransitions is characterized by high entropy, but does not simply exhibit disorder.
elements.sent-2411-022: Unlike conventional phase transitions, this state is a frustrated quasiphase that does not mix with the low-temperature low-entropy state at the microscopic level.
elements.sent-2411-023: Therefore, the presence of frustration is a crucial condition for the occurrence of pseudotransitions in the system.
elements.sent-2411-024: Previous studies of pseudotransitions have focused on exploring complex frustrated (quasi)-one-dimensional systems, including decorated chains~\cite{Rojas2019PRE,Krokhmalskii2021PhysicaA such as diamondlike chains~\cite{Hovhannisyan2016JPCM,Carvalho2018JMMM,Carvalho2019AP , ladders~\cite{Rojas2016SSC,Hutak2021PLA , tubes~\cite{Strecka2016JMMM , double-tetrahedral chains~\cite{galisova2015vigorous,Rojas2020JPCM , and hexagonal nanowires~\cite{Pimenta2022JMMM .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Additionally, pseudotransitions have been observed in anisotropic Potts models with $q$ states~\cite{Panov2021PRE,Panov2023PRE .}
elements.sent-2411-026: In general, the study of these models addresses the theoretical framework necessary for understanding a rapidly growing class of materials known as single-chain magnets~\cite{Coulon2006,Zhang2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: Actually, the source of frustrations in spin chains can be impurities.
elements.sent-2411-028: As a source of nonmagnetic impurities, in addition to chemical substitution, it is important to mention the photoinduced change of the magnetic state of ions in single-chain magnets~\cite{Liu2013 , as well as the charge disproportionation reaction that is characteristic of a wide class of magnets~\cite{Moskvin2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-029: However, it has not been previously observed that pseudotransitions can occur in dilute chains with impurities.
elements.sent-2411-030: The absence of pseudotransitions in the dilute Ising chain was earlier proved~\cite{Panov2022PRE .}
elements.sent-2411-031: We assume that the lack of pseudotransitions in this dilute system is explained by the absence of frustrated phases with the necessary properties.
elements.sent-2411-032: In this work, we investigate an Ising spin chain that is diluted with nonmagnetic charged impurities of two types.
elements.sent-2411-033: These impurities are mobile and subject to density-density interactions.
elements.sent-2411-034: A two-dimensional version of this model was considered in~\cite{Panov2017JLTP,Panov2017JETPlett,Panov2019JMMM,Panov2019JSNM,Yasinskaya2020PSS,Yasinskaya2020APPA,Yasinskaya2021PSS,Yasinskaya2022IEEE , as an atomic limit of a more general model for cuprates~\cite{Moskvin2011PRB,Moskvin2015JETP,Panov2019PMM,Moskvin2020PSS,Moskvin2021CM,Moskvin2022JMMM,Moskvin2022JPCS .}}
elements.sent-2411-035: Despite the simplified nature of the spin-spin interaction, this model was used to describe the competition of charge and magnetic ordering in the normal phase of cuprates.
elements.sent-2411-036: The valence multiplet of the many-electron states of the $[$CuO$_{4 ]^{7-,6-,5-}$ centers, or nominally Cu$^{1+,2+,3+}$, corresponds to the three components of the pseudospin triplet.}
elements.sent-2411-037: The spin singlets Cu$^{1+}$ and Cu$^{3+}$ play the role of nonmagnetic centers having different charges and subject to density-density correlations, while the magnetic spin-$1/2$ centers of Cu$^{2+}$ are bound by antiferromagnetic superexchange interaction.
elements.sent-2411-038: Note that a similar model has been used for the one-dimensional cuprate chains~\cite{Strecka2024EPJB , which can serve as a prototype for one-dimensional cuprates such as SrCuO$_{2}$~\cite{Bounoua2017 .}}
elements.sent-2411-039: The peculiarity of our model is accounting for the condition of constant charge concentration in the system.
elements.sent-2411-040: This not only requires the use of specialized techniques to calculate thermodynamic properties, but also results in novel features of pseudotransitions.
elements.sent-2411-041: Similar to systems with a conserved order parameter~\cite{Yang1952PR,Newman , the lattice hardcore bosons or the extended Hubbard models~\cite{Batrouni2000PRL,Kapcia2012JPCM,Kapcia2013JPCM,Kapcia2013JSNM,Kapcia2015JSNM,Kapcia2015PhysicaA,Kapcia2017PRE , our model exhibits various forms of phase separation.}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Analytical treatment of phase separation in real systems is a complex task, and often involves the use of phenomenological assumptions, such as Maxwell construction~\cite{Plischke2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-043: The study of our one-dimensional model allows us to assess the validity of using and the limits of applicability of such constructions.
elements.sent-2411-044: The paper is structured as follows.
elements.sent-2411-045: Section~II presents the spin-pseudospin model of a dilute Ising chain and specifics of the calculation methods.
elements.sent-2411-046: Properties of the ground state phases are discussed in Section~III, and the most interesting results about conventional pseudo-transitions in a ``pure'' chain and the previously unobserved type of pseudotransitions in a dilute chain, along with their interconnections, are presented in Section~IV.
elements.sent-2411-047: Section~V summarizes our key findings and draws conclusions.
elements.sent-2411-048: Some additional details are given in the Appendix.

elements.section-2411-003: Ground state properties: diagrams, entropy and phase boundaries
elements.sent-2411-087: Let us now proceed to the discussion of a wide variety of ground state phases in a dilute Ising magnet.
elements.sent-2411-088: All the ground states are presented in Table~\ref{phtab with their energies and corresponding chain state illustrations.}
elements.sent-2411-089: These ground states collectively form the ground state phase diagrams (GSPD) shown in Fig.~\ref{GSPD in the $(\Delta,J)$ plane for three cases of $n$: (a) ``pure'', $n=0$; (b) weak dilution, $0 < \left| n \right| < 1/2$; and (c) strong dilution, $ \left| n \right| \geq 1/2$.}

elements.sent-2411-143: Let us briefly discuss the prospective impact of the magnetic field on the ground state properties.
elements.sent-2411-144: First of all, a strong magnetic field facilitates the emergence of a new ground state phase.
elements.sent-2411-145: In this phase, spins are aligned with the magnetic field, alternating with nonmagnetic impurities.
elements.sent-2411-146: Consequently, impurities and spins do not have neighbors of the same type.
elements.sent-2411-147: This phase is inherently frustrated, even in a ``pure'' state with $n=0$, and is realized only for weak dilution case $0 \leq \left| n \right| < 1/2$.
elements.sent-2411-148: This phase borders the CO phase, effectively excluding the triple point CO-FM-AFM for $n=0$ and the region of pseudotransitions with $V > \left| J \right|$; therefore, pseudotransitions will be observed only when $\left| J \right| > V$.
elements.sent-2411-149: Second, the introduction of a magnetic field will result in the displacement of certain phase boundaries, which will affect the positioning and pseudocritical temperatures of pseudotransitions.
elements.sent-2411-150: Significantly, the magnetic field nontrivially affects the structure of the spin chain.
elements.sent-2411-151: It can change the type of a periodic Markov chain corresponding to the magnetic ground state, and potentially lead to the emergence of sublattice long-range order~\cite{Panov2023PRE,Yasinskaya2024PSS .}
elements.sent-2411-152: However, it should be emphasized that the presence of a magnetic field is not imperative for the main focus of this study.
elements.section-2411-004: Thermodynamics of a dilute Ising model
elements.sent-2411-153: Next, let us delve into a comprehensive analysis of the temperature properties of the ``pure'' system at $n = 0$ and focus on the emergence of pseudotransitions in this scenario.
elements.sent-2411-154: As outlined in the previous section, we anticipate pseudotransitions to occur near the boundary between the charge and magnetic phases based on the entropy criterion, with the exception of the critical point $V=\left|J\right|$.
elements.sent-2411-155: The range of parameters where pseudotransitions are observed depends on the proximity to this critical point, with range becoming significantly narrower at $V \sim \left| J \right|$, so that capturing a pseudotransition in proximity to the critical point proves to be challenging in practice.
elements.sent-2411-156: However, in extreme cases where $V$ significantly outweighs $J$ or vice versa, the pseudotransition becomes apparent from the side of the CO or (A)FM phases, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-157: This will be demonstrated by examining the temperature dependencies of thermodynamic quantities.
elements.sent-2411-158: Our findings reveal that the pseudotransitions are identical in the two symmetric regions, $V \gg \left| J \right|$ and $\left| J \right| \gg V$, for $\delta \varepsilon_{0 > 0$ and $\delta \varepsilon_{0 < 0$, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-159: For instance, consider two symmetrical situations, with $V = 10 \left| J \right| = 1$ and $\left| J \right| = 10 V = 1$.
elements.sent-2411-160: This scenario is illustrated in Fig.~\ref{ps1 , which displays the temperature dependencies of the grand potential, entropy, specific heat, correlation length, order parameter, and corresponding susceptibility for different values of $\delta \varepsilon_{0}$, but one must take $\delta \varepsilon_{0 > 0$ for $V \gg \left| J \right|$, and $\delta \varepsilon_{0 < 0$ for $V \ll \left| J \right|$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-161: The curves will be identical for these two ratios.
elements.sent-2411-162: Furthermore, for $\delta \varepsilon_{0 > 0$ the order parameter $\mathcal{O}$ will be $M_\text{CO}$.}
elements.sent-2411-163: In contrast, for $\delta \varepsilon_{0 < 0$, it is necessary to use $m_\text{FM}$ for $J < 0$ and $m_\text{AFM}$ for $J > 0$.}
elements.sent-2411-164: It is important to note that for the FM case, the system must be considered in a small magnetic field $h \rightarrow 0$ (without loss of generality, we have set $h = 10^{-5}$) to provide the magnetically biased phase.
elements.sent-2411-165: Otherwise, the magnetization would be zero when $h=0$.
elements.sent-2411-166: A high-entropy state at the boundary is characterized by a smooth decrease in entropy to zero with temperature [as illustrated by the green curve in Fig.~\ref{ps1 (b)).}
elements.sent-2411-167: States in close proximity to the boundary exhibit a sudden transition to a low-entropy state, which becomes increasingly sharp as it approaches the phase boundary.
elements.sent-2411-168: This results in a narrow pseudotransition region, where the grand potential shows a breakpoint, and both entropy and order parameter exhibit sudden jumps, which resemble a typical first-order phase transition.
elements.sent-2411-169: Nevertheless, there are no discontinuities, and all quantities remain as smooth, analytical functions.
elements.sent-2411-170: During this pseudotransition, a transition takes place to the quasiphase that will become the ground state at $T=0$ under these parameters.
elements.sent-2411-171: Moving farther away from the phase boundary causes the pseudotransition to occur at higher temperatures, with the grand potential and entropy becoming showing a more gradual change.

elements.sent-2411-177: The expression for pseudocritical temperature can be derived from the square root part of transfer-matrix eigenvalues.
elements.sent-2411-178: Assuming $h=0$ and $\mu = 0$, we can get the expressions for $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$, similarly to those defined in~\cite{Souza2018SSC : \begin{equation}\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{1}{2} \left(w_1 + w_2 \pm \sqrt{\left(w_1 - w_2 \right)^2 + 4 w_0^2}\right),\end{equation} where \begin{equation}w_1 = 2 \cosh \frac{J}{T}, \quad w_2 = 2 e^{-\frac{\Delta}{T}} \cosh \frac{V}{T},\quad w_0 = e^{-\frac{\Delta}{2 T}}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-179: The pseudotransition point is determined by the condition $w_1 = w_2 $.
elements.sent-2411-180: In this case, the eigenvalues $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$ will converge as close as possible.
elements.sent-2411-181: Consequently, we arrive at a transcendental equation that defines the temperature of the pseudotransition: \begin{equation}\frac{\Delta}{T_p} = \ln \frac{\cosh \left(V/T_{p}\right)}{\cosh \left(J/T_{p}\right)}. \label{eq:Tp1}\end{equation} It is apparent from this expression that there is no pseudotransition for $V=\left| J \right|$.

elements.sent-2411-183: Thus, near the triple point CO--FM--AFM, on the line $J = 0$, the analytical expression for $T_p$ is provided by the following expression: \begin{equation}T_p = \frac{V-\Delta}{\ln 2}. \label{Tp}\end{equation} Fig.~\ref{cxi(tau) displays the temperature dependencies of the specific heat and correlation length on a logarithmic scale in the vicinity of the pseudocritical temperature $\tau = T-T_p$ for the same set of parameters.}

elements.sent-2411-230: Following the analogy with Ehrenfest classification, the transitions discussed in the previous section were termed ``first-order'' pseudotransitions, as indicated in the section heading.

elements.sent-2411-236: To determine the temperatures of ``second-order'' pseudotransitions, we will employ the Maxwell construction~\cite{Plischke2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-237: Assuming the presence of two coexisting macroscopic homogeneous phases 1 and 2, the free energy of the phase separation state per node is expressed as \begin{equation}f_{\text{PS} } = p f_1 (n_1) + (1 - p) f_2 (n_2), \label{eq:fPS}\end{equation} where $p$ is the system fraction with density $n_1$, $1 - p$ is the system fraction with density $n_2$, so that $p n_1 + (1 - p) n_2 = n$.

elements.sent-2411-279: In the present article, we have addressed the study of a one-dimensional spin chain diluted by two types of charged nonmagnetic impurities.
elements.sent-2411-280: The peculiarity of the model is the condition of constant charge density of the system $n$ with a possible variable number of impurities of each type and spin centers.
elements.sent-2411-281: To accurately calculate thermodynamic averages, we introduce a high-performance method that utilizes the properties of the transfer-matrix of the system.
elements.sent-2411-282: Assuming a fixed charge density, the ground state phase diagram includes phases that do not form a mixed state at the phase boundaries.
elements.sent-2411-283: According to the Rojas criterion~\cite{Rojas2020BJP,*Rojas2020APPA , pseudotransitions can be observed near these boundaries.}
elements.sent-2411-284: These pseudotransitions are characterized by striking features of the specific heat, correlation length, order parameter, and susceptibility at some finite temperature.
elements.sent-2411-285: Thus, at $n=0$ near the boundary between CO and (A)FM phases, pseudotransitions are observed both in the CO phase region for $V>\left|J\right|$, as was previously discussed in~\cite{Strecka2024EPJB , and in the AFM phase region for $V<\left|J\right|$.}
elements.sent-2411-286: This highlights the inherent symmetry of the spin and pseudospin systems.
elements.sent-2411-287: These pseudotransitions exhibit known features, such as characteristic values of pseudocritical exponents, and are referred to as ``first-order'' pseudotransitions.
elements.sent-2411-288: At $n\neq0$, the ``second-order'' pseudotransitions are observed in the system.
elements.sent-2411-289: These are characterized by a distinct breakpoint in the temperature dependency of the entropy and order parameter, which is different from a stepwise behavior observed in the ``first-order'' pseudotransitions.
elements.sent-2411-290: Furthermore, they exhibit sharp, finite jumps in the specific heat and magnetic susceptibility, akin to the behavior observed in conventional second-order phase transitions.
elements.sent-2411-291: We show that there is a continuous transformation of the type of pseudotransitions, transitioning from the ``first-order'' at $n = 0$ to the ``second-order'' as $| n |$ grows.
elements.sent-2411-292: The specificity of CO and (A)FM states to alternate during the pseudotransition allows us to plot the temperature dependency of the fraction of the (A)FM phase.
elements.sent-2411-293: The pseudotransition at $n = 0$ is characterized by an abrupt change in the state of the entire chain.
elements.sent-2411-294: In the ``second-order'' pseudotransition, the (A)FM phase appears in the form of nuclei, and beyond $| n | > 0.15$, the fraction of the (A)FM phase increases linearly with decreasing temperature.
elements.sent-2411-295: In this case, it is possible to describe with high accuracy the thermodynamic properties of the system in the framework of a simple phenomenological Maxwell construction for two quasiphases, dilute CO and (A)FM.
elements.sent-2411-296: This enables us to find phenomenological dependencies of the ``second-order'' pseudotransition temperature and the fraction of the (A)FM phase, which, for $| n | > 0.15$, completely determines the behavior of the system below the pseudotransition point.
elements.sent-2411-297: The inapplicability of the phenomenological description for $| n | < 0.15$ is attributed to the absence of the dilute CO quasiphase formation up to the pseudotransition point, as evident from the temperature dependency of the system's entropy.
elements.sent-2411-298: A comprehensive examination of the one-dimensional model facilitates a comparison between accurate calculations and reasonable phenomenological constructions, aiding in the comprehension of the peculiarities of physical properties and the limits of phenomenological applicability.
elements.sent-2411-299: This allows us to use this experience in more complex systems exhibiting phase separation.

elements.sent-2411-301: Eigenvalues $\{\lambda_i, \, i=1, \ldots 4\}$ of the transfer-matrix~(\ref{TM ) are determined by solving the equation \begin{equation}\vert \hat{T} - \lambda \hat{I} \vert = \lambda^4 + a \lambda^3 + b \lambda^2 + c \lambda + d = 0, \label{lambda_eq}\end{equation} where $\hat{I}$ is an identity matrix, \begin{eqnarray}\!\!\! a = -2 e^{-\frac{J}{T}}-2 e^{-\frac{\Delta +V}{T}} \cosh \frac{\mu }{T} , \!\!\! b = -4 e^{-\frac{\Delta+V+J}{T}} \left( e^{\frac{V+J}{T}} - 1 \right) \cosh \frac{\mu}{T} \!\!\! - 2 \sinh \frac{2J}{T} -2 e^{-\frac{2\Delta}{T}} \sinh \frac{2V}{T} , \!\!\! c = 8 e^{-\frac{\Delta}{T}} \sinh \frac{J}{T} \cosh \frac{\mu}{T} \left( e^{-\frac{V}{T}} \cosh \frac{J}{T} -1 \right) \!\!\! {}+8 e^{-\frac{2\Delta}{T}} \sinh \frac{V}{T} \left( e^{-\frac{J}{T}} \cosh \frac{V}{T} -1 \right) \! , \!\!\! d = 16 e^{-\frac{2\Delta}{T}} \!\! \left( \! \cosh \frac{V}{T} \cosh \frac{J}{T} -1 \! \right) \sinh \frac{V}{T} \sinh \frac{J}{T} . \label{coef}\end{eqnarray} Solutions of equation~\eqref{lambda_eq for eigenvalues of transfer-matrix can be derived using Ferrari's method~\cite{Korn2000 : \begin{equation}\lambda_{1,2,3,4} = -\frac{a}{4} + \frac{\pm_s \sqrt{\alpha+2y} \pm_t \sqrt{-(3 \alpha + 2y \pm_s \frac{2 \beta}{\sqrt{\alpha+2y}})}}{2},\end{equation} where \begin{eqnarray}\alpha = - \frac{3a^2}{8} + b , \beta = \frac{a^3}{8} - \frac{a b}{2} + c , \gamma = - \frac{3 a^4}{256} + \frac{a^2 b}{16} - \frac{a c}{4} + d, p = -\frac{\alpha^2}{12} - \gamma, q = - \frac{\alpha^3}{108} + \frac{\alpha \gamma}{3} - \frac{\beta^2}{8} , r = - \frac{q}{2} + \sqrt{\frac{q^2}{4}+\frac{p^3}{27}} , u = \sqrt[3]{r} , y = -\frac{5}{6} \alpha + u + \begin{cases} -\sqrt[3]{q}, u = 0 ; - \frac{p}{3u}, u \neq 0 . \end{cases}\end{eqnarray} Here $\pm_s$ and $\pm_t$ are two independent parameters, each of which is equal to either $+$ or $-$.}}}
